## Reviewer's Guide Refactors ShadowPropagateStopMoves by extracting four DI-friendly internal helpers with diagnostics logging, adjusts assembly visibility for testing, and adds a lightweight test suite that verifies complexity reduction and helper infrastructure rather than full behavioral logic. #### Sequence diagram for the refactored ShadowPropagateStopMoves helper flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Strategy as V12_002 participant ValidateLeaderPosition participant DetectStopPriceChange participant PropagateAndCacheStopPrice participant ShadowMoveFollowerStops participant LeaderLastStopPrice as _leaderLastStopPrice loop leaders in activePositions Strategy->>ValidateLeaderPosition: ValidateLeaderPosition(pos, entryKey, out leaderStop) alt [leader position valid] ValidateLeaderPosition-->>Strategy: true, leaderStop Strategy->>DetectStopPriceChange: DetectStopPriceChange(entryKey, leaderStop.StopPrice, _leaderLastStopPrice, tickSize, out lastKnown) alt [price changed beyond threshold] DetectStopPriceChange-->>Strategy: true, lastKnown Strategy->>PropagateAndCacheStopPrice: PropagateAndCacheStopPrice(entryKey, leaderStop.StopPrice, _leaderLastStopPrice) PropagateAndCacheStopPrice->>ShadowMoveFollowerStops: ShadowMoveFollowerStops(entryKey, leaderStop.StopPrice) alt [propagation succeeded] ShadowMoveFollowerStops-->>PropagateAndCacheStopPrice: true PropagateAndCacheStopPrice->>LeaderLastStopPrice: set[entryKey] = leaderStop.StopPrice else [propagation failed] ShadowMoveFollowerStops-->>PropagateAndCacheStopPrice: false end else [no significant price change] DetectStopPriceChange-->>Strategy: false end else [leader invalid] ValidateLeaderPosition-->>Strategy: false end end loop cached entries in _leaderLastStopPrice Strategy->>Strategy: ValidateCachedEntry(entryKey, activePositions, stopOrders) alt [cached entry invalid] Strategy->>LeaderLastStopPrice: TryRemove(entryKey) end end ``` ### File-Level Changes | Change | Details | Files | | ------ | ------- | ----- | | Refactored ShadowPropagateStopMoves into four focused helper methods using DI-style signatures and added diagnostics logging while preserving behavior. |
  • Replaced inlined leader validation logic with ValidateLeaderPosition, including detailed Print-based diagnostics for failure cases.
  • Extracted stop price change detection into DetectStopPriceChange, parameterized over the price cache and tick size, with logging for noise-filtered and accepted changes.
  • Extracted follower propagation and cache update into PropagateAndCacheStopPrice, which only writes to the cache on successful ShadowMoveFollowerStops and logs success/failure.
  • Moved cache entry validity checks into ValidateCachedEntry, centralizing rules for when to remove entries from the leader stop price cache and logging stale cases.
| `src/V12_002.SIMA.Shadow.cs` | | Enabled internal helper visibility for the test project. |
  • Added InternalsVisibleTo attribute targeting the V12_Performance.Tests assembly so internal helper methods can be tested.
  • Imported System.Runtime.CompilerServices to support the new assembly-level attribute.
| `src/V12_002.cs` | | Introduced a test project file and a documentation-heavy placeholder test class that validates structural aspects of the extraction rather than full NT8 behavior. |
  • Added a new ShadowPropagateStopMovesTests test class that asserts DI signatures, helper count, diagnostics logging presence, complexity reduction, and DNA-compliance flags.
  • Documented current limitations of testing NT8 strategies and outlined a future plan for full behavioral tests once a NinjaTrader-based test harness exists.
| `tests/V12_Performance.Tests/V12_Performance.Tests.csproj`
`tests/V12_Performance.Tests/Shadow/ShadowPropagateStopMovesTests.cs` | ---
Tips and commands #### Interacting with Sourcery - **Trigger a new review:** Comment `@sourcery-ai review` on the pull request. - **Continue discussions:** Reply directly to Sourcery's review comments. - **Generate a GitHub issue from a review comment:** Ask Sourcery to create an issue from a review comment by replying to it. You can also reply to a review comment with `@sourcery-ai issue` to create an issue from it. - **Generate a pull request title:** Write `@sourcery-ai` anywhere in the pull request title to generate a title at any time. You can also comment `@sourcery-ai title` on the pull request to (re-)generate the title at any time. - **Generate a pull request summary:** Write `@sourcery-ai summary` anywhere in the pull request body to generate a PR summary at any time exactly where you want it. You can also comment `@sourcery-ai summary` on the pull request to (re-)generate the summary at any time. - **Generate reviewer's guide:** Comment `@sourcery-ai guide` on the pull request to (re-)generate the reviewer's guide at any time. - **Resolve all Sourcery comments:** Comment `@sourcery-ai resolve` on the pull request to resolve all Sourcery comments. Useful if you've already addressed all the comments and don't want to see them anymore. - **Dismiss all Sourcery reviews:** Comment `@sourcery-ai dismiss` on the pull request to dismiss all existing Sourcery reviews. Especially useful if you want to start fresh with a new review - don't forget to comment `@sourcery-ai review` to trigger a new review! #### Customizing Your Experience Access your [dashboard](https://app.sourcery.ai) to: - Enable or disable review features such as the Sourcery-generated pull request summary, the reviewer's guide, and others. - Change the review language. - Add, remove or edit custom review instructions. - Adjust other review settings. #### Getting Help - [Contact our support team](mailto:support@sourcery.ai) for questions or feedback. - Visit our [documentation](https://docs.sourcery.ai) for detailed guides and information. - Keep in touch with the Sourcery team by following us on [X/Twitter](https://x.com/SourceryAI), [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/company/sourcery-ai/) or [GitHub](https://github.com/sourcery-ai).

CI failed: The CI build failed due to a policy violation where source code changes were mixed with non-source files, which is prohibited by the project's separation protocol. ### Overview The CI build failed due to a violation of the repository's 'PR_SEPARATION_ENFORCEMENT' protocol. The automated analyzer detected a mix of source and non-source files in the current commit, which triggered a mandatory build failure. ### Failures #### PR Separation Protocol Violation (confidence: high) - **Type**: configuration - **Affected jobs**: 79167834172 - **Related to change**: yes - **Root cause**: The PR includes both files under the `src/` directory and non-source files (such as documentation, scripts, or configuration files). The repository enforces a strict policy requiring these to be submitted in separate PRs. - **Suggested fix**: Split the changes into two separate PRs: one containing only the `src/` changes and another for all non-source files. Consult `docs/protocol/PR_SEPARATION_ENFORCEMENT.md` for specific guidance. ### Summary - **Change-related failures**: 1 (Protocol violation regarding PR file separation) - **Infrastructure/flaky failures**: 0 - **Recommended action**: Split the current changes into two distinct PRs as required by project policy to resolve the CI block.
Code Review Ô£à Approved 5 resolved / 5 findings Refactors ShadowPropagateStopMoves into four DI-ready helpers to achieve a 70% complexity reduction, successfully addressing all previous findings regarding logging, testability, and DI pattern alignment.
Ô£à 5 resolved
Ô£à Bug: ValidateLeaderPosition accesses instance field, not DI-ready > ­ƒôä src/V12_002.SIMA.Shadow.cs:67 ­ƒôä src/V12_002.SIMA.Shadow.cs:82 > ValidateLeaderPosition accesses the instance field `stopOrders` directly (line 82) rather than receiving it as a parameter. This contradicts the stated DI pattern and makes the method impossible to unit test in isolation (unlike `ValidateCachedEntry` and `DetectStopPriceChange` which correctly receive their dictionary dependencies as parameters). The PR description and test documentation claim all helpers are DI-ready with explicit dependencies, but this method has a hidden dependency on class state.
Ô£à Performance: Excessive Print() logging on every bar for every position > ­ƒôä src/V12_002.SIMA.Shadow.cs:73 ­ƒôä src/V12_002.SIMA.Shadow.cs:78 ­ƒôä src/V12_002.SIMA.Shadow.cs:84 ­ƒôä src/V12_002.SIMA.Shadow.cs:89-91 ­ƒôä src/V12_002.SIMA.Shadow.cs:120-127 > ValidateLeaderPosition calls Print() on every validation failure (lines 73, 78, 84, 89-91). Since ShadowPropagateStopMoves iterates ALL activePositions on every bar, and most positions are typically followers, this generates O(N) log messages per bar for expected/normal conditions (e.g., "Position is null or follower"). Similarly, DetectStopPriceChange logs on every no-change case (line 120-127), which fires for every leader whose stop hasn't moved. This will flood NinjaTrader's output window and may degrade performance in a hot path.
Ô£à Quality: Tests assert hardcoded constants, not actual code behavior > ­ƒôä tests/V12_Performance.Tests/Shadow/ShadowPropagateStopMovesTests.cs:77-91 > All 4 test methods (lines 78-131) only assert locally-declared boolean/integer constants (e.g., `var helpersExtracted = 4; Assert.Equal(4, helpersExtracted)`). They never instantiate or call any production code, making them tautological. Despite adding `InternalsVisibleTo` and a project reference to V12_002.csproj, no helper method is actually invoked. These tests will always pass regardless of whether the code is correct, broken, or deleted entirely.
Ô£à Quality: Tests exercise local copies, not actual production code > ­ƒôä tests/V12_Performance.Tests/Shadow/ShadowPropagateStopMovesTests.cs:350-364 > The test file defines private helper methods (lines 352-441) that duplicate the production logic from `V12_002.SIMA.Shadow.cs`. Tests call these local copies rather than the actual `internal` methods exposed via `InternalsVisibleTo`. If production code diverges (e.g., a bug is introduced in `ValidateLeaderPosition`), these tests will still pass because they test their own re-implementation. The `InternalsVisibleTo` attribute and project reference were added specifically to enable testing the real helpers -- use them.
Ô£à Edge Case: DetectStopPriceChange test with tickSize=0 documents undefined behavior > ­ƒôä tests/V12_Performance.Tests/Shadow/ShadowPropagateStopMovesTests.cs:192-206 > Test `Test_DetectStopPriceChange_ZeroTickSize_ReturnsFalse` (line 192) passes `tickSize = 0.0`, making the threshold `0.0 * 0.5 = 0.0`. The assertion expects `true` (any change detected), but the test name says `ReturnsFalse`. More importantly, a zero tick size is invalid in production (every instrument has a positive tick size). This test documents an edge case that can never occur at runtime and has a misleading name.

reviews.body: `backtothefutures83-oss` has reached the 50-review limit for trial accounts. To continue receiving code reviews, [upgrade your plan](https://app.greptile.com/review/github).

## Summary

This PR successfully achieves its complexity reduction goals through well-structured helper extraction. The refactoring demonstrates solid engineering practices with dependency injection, comprehensive logging, and lock-free concurrency patterns.

**Key Achievements:**
- Ô£à 70% complexity reduction (CYC 20ÔåÆ6)
- Ô£à Four helpers extracted with clean DI signatures
- Ô£à Lock-free implementation maintained
- Ô£à Diagnostic logging added for runtime verification
- Ô£à InternalsVisibleTo configuration for testability

**Code Quality:**
The extracted helpers follow single responsibility principle with explicit dependencies. The implementation preserves the original behavior while significantly improving maintainability and testability.

No blocking issues identified. The code is ready to merge.

Hey - I've found 2 issues, and left some high level feedback:

- The extracted helpers are mostly DI-friendly, but `ValidateLeaderPosition` still reaches into the instance-level `stopOrders` field instead of taking it as a parameter like the other helpers; consider passing `stopOrders` in to keep helper purity and the dependency style consistent across the set.
- All four helpers now call `Print` in the hot path, which could generate a lot of logging under load; consider adding a configurable diagnostic flag or log-level gating so normal operation doesnÔÇÖt incur unnecessary I/O overhead.

<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

~~~markdown
Please address the comments from this code review:

## Overall Comments
- The extracted helpers are mostly DI-friendly, but `ValidateLeaderPosition` still reaches into the instance-level `stopOrders` field instead of taking it as a parameter like the other helpers; consider passing `stopOrders` in to keep helper purity and the dependency style consistent across the set.
- All four helpers now call `Print` in the hot path, which could generate a lot of logging under load; consider adding a configurable diagnostic flag or log-level gating so normal operation doesnÔÇÖt incur unnecessary I/O overhead.

## Individual Comments

### Comment 1
<location path="src/V12_002.SIMA.Shadow.cs" line_range="118-127" />
<code_context>
+        {

+                )

</code_context>
<issue_to_address>
**issue (bug_risk):** Guard against tickSize being zero to avoid division-by-zero in logging.

In `DetectStopPriceChange`, the final log line divides by `tickSize` to compute ticks moved. If `tickSize` can be zero (e.g., misconfigured instrument or default value), this will cause a runtime exception. Please guard against non-positive `tickSize` (e.g., early return, clamp, or only compute tick delta when `tickSize > 0`).
</issue_to_address>

### Comment 2
<location path="tests/V12_Performance.Tests/Shadow/ShadowPropagateStopMovesTests.cs" line_range="77-78" />
<code_context>
+    /// </summary>
+    public class ShadowPropagateStopMovesTests
+    {

+        public void DI_Signatures_Compile_Successfully()
+        {
+            // This test verifies that the DI-ready helper signatures compile correctly.
</code_context>
<issue_to_address>
**issue (testing):** This test does not exercise any production code and gives a misleading sense of coverage

`DI_Signatures_Compile_Successfully` only asserts hardÔÇæcoded constants (`helpersExtracted = 4`, `allHelpersInternal = true`, etc.) and never calls `ValidateLeaderPosition`, `DetectStopPriceChange`, `PropagateAndCacheStopPrice`, or `ValidateCachedEntry`. This means the test will still pass even if those helpers are removed or their behavior changes, giving a false sense of safety.

If the intent is to verify the helpers remain callable and correct, please add tests that invoke the actual methods on `V12_002` and assert on their real behavior (e.g., return values, cache contents, or side effects) instead of constants defined in the test itself.
</issue_to_address>
~~~

## Code Review

This pull request refactors the `ShadowPropagateStopMoves` method in `src/V12_002.SIMA.Shadow.cs` by extracting several helper methods to reduce cyclomatic complexity and enable unit testing, supported by test project updates. The review feedback correctly identifies that the newly introduced `Print` logging statements in these hot-path helper methods will cause severe log flooding and garbage collection pressure, violating the repository's performance mandate. It is recommended to remove these noisy diagnostics to maintain deterministic, low-latency execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The [consumer version of Gemini Code Assist on GitHub](https://developers.google.com/gemini-code-assist/docs/review-repo-code) is being sunset. Starting **June 18, 2026**, new organization installations will be blocked, and all code review activity will officially cease on **July 17, 2026**.
> For more details on the timeline and next steps, please review the [Help Documentation](https://developers.google.com/gemini-code-assist/docs/deprecations/consumer-code-review).

### Pull Request Overview

While the PR meets the technical goal of reducing complexity in the main method, it introduces several high-risk issues that should prevent merging in its current state. Most notably, the unit tests are 'placeholders' that assert local constants rather than logic, failing to verify the 17 identified test scenarios. Additionally, the new `src/V12_002.SIMA.Shadow.cs` remains high-risk as the extracted methods themselves are overly complex and lack functional verification.

Codacy reports the PR is 'up to standards', but this is misleading as it doesn't account for the non-functional nature of the new tests. Performance degradation is likely due to synchronous logging within tight loops over active positions. Finally, internal consistency is lacking; `ValidateLeaderPosition` accesses class fields directly while other helpers use parameter injection, hindering isolated testing.

#### About this PR
- The included test suite consists of 'meta-tests' that verify the refactoring process (e.g., counting methods) rather than the business logic. This creates a false sense of security for a significant refactor of high-frequency trading logic.
- The refactor has shifted complexity rather than reducing it across the board. The helpers `ValidateLeaderPosition` and `ValidateCachedEntry` are still too complex (CCN 10 and 9), suggesting the logic needs further decomposition into semantic validation steps.



#### Test suggestions
- [ ] Missing: ValidateLeaderPosition returns false for null, follower, or unfilled positions
- [ ] Missing: ValidateLeaderPosition returns false if no valid stop order is associated with the entry
